elements.sent-2411-003: Following the pivotal work of Sevastyanov \cite{Sevastyanov-57 , who considered branching processes with homogeneous Poisson immigration, much has been done to understand the behaviour of such processes under different types of branching and immigration mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-004: Recently, the case where the times of immigration are generated by a non-homogeneous Poisson process was considered in depth.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we try to demonstrate how one can use the framework of point processes in order to go beyond the Poisson process.
elements.sent-2411-006: As an illustration, we show how to transfer techniques from the case of Poisson immigration to the case where it is spanned by a determinantal point process.
elements.sent-2411-007: Keywords: Branching processes with immigration, Point processes.
elements.sent-2411-009: The main purpose of this paper is to examine the impact of immigration on behaviour of the continuous-time Markov branching processes, both single- and multi-type, focussing on immigration types that differ from the Poisson model.
elements.sent-2411-010: Although classical Poisson processes are the most commonly used to model the immigration component, they have certain limitations in practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-011: These include the assumption of equidispersion, which may not always hold, and their time-homogeneity, which is addressed by introducing time-non-homogeneous intensity rates.
elements.sent-2411-012: Recently, such branching models with immigration at the jump times of a non-homogeneous Poisson process were considered in depth by Mitov et al. \cite{Mitov-Yanev-Hyrien-18-critical-multi in the critical case and later on by Slavtchova-Bojkova et al. \cite{ Bojkova-Hyrien-Yanev-22, Bojkova-Hyrien-Yanev-23 in the non-critical cases.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In these studies, the efforts were concentrated mainly on analysing the asymptotic behaviour of the processes for various rates of the Poisson measure, under the assumption that these rates are asymptotically equivalent to either exponential or regularly varying functions.
elements.sent-2411-014: Consequently, results similar to the strong law of large numbers and central limit theorems were established.
elements.sent-2411-015: Multi-type Markov branching processes were first introduced by Kolmogorov and Dmitriev \cite{Kolmogorov-Dmitriev-47 , marking the term branching process as one of the earliest concepts in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The notion of branching processes with immigration was later formalised by Sevastyanov \cite{Sevastyanov-57 , who explored a single-type Markov process with immigration driven by a time-homogeneous Poisson process, and derived limiting distributions for subcritical, supercritical, and critical cases.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Since then, numerous extensions of branching processes with immigration have been developed and thoroughly explored.
elements.sent-2411-018: Seminal reviews by Sevastyanov \cite{Sevastyanov-68 , and Vatutin and Zubkov \cite{Vatutin-Zubkov-87,Vatutin_AZ_93 , have highlighted many key results.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: More recent advancements have been made by Barczy et al. \cite{Barczy1,Barczy2 , González et al. \cite{Gonzalez-Kersting-Minuesa-19 , and Li et al. \cite{Li-Vatutin-Zhang-21 , among others, who have expanded the theory of these processes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Branching processes with time-non-homogeneous immigration were first introduced by Durham \cite{Durham-71 and Foster and Williamson \cite{Foster-Williamson-71 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Comprehensive reviews of these models can be found in the monographs by Badalbaev et al. \cite{BadRah and Rahimov \cite{Rahimov-95 , as well as in Rahimov’s more recent review paper \cite{Rahimov-21 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In the recent work of Rabehasaina and Woo \cite{Rabehasaina-21 , the model described in Mitov et al. \cite{Mitov-Yanev-Hyrien-18-critical-multi and Slavtchova-Bojkova et al. \cite{Bojkova-Hyrien-Yanev-22, Bojkova-Hyrien-Yanev-23 were considered and the established limit results were acquired by means of characteristic functions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this work, we aim to introduce a general framework for the Laplace transforms of random point processes within the context of branching processes.
elements.sent-2411-024: We believe that this approach will be valuable for researchers looking to extend their work beyond the Poisson process.
elements.sent-2411-025: For example, our method simplifies the derivation of the generating function for a branching process with immigration, which often serves as the foundation for many studies.
elements.sent-2411-026: Traditionally, this derivation was based on the exact distribution of points over a given interval, which is tractable in the case of a Poisson process, as demonstrated in \cite[Theorem 1]{Mitov-Yanev-Hyrien-18-critical-multi , \cite[Lemma 3.1]{Rabehasaina-21 , and \cite[Theorem 1]{Butkovsky-12 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Our framework provides a more unified and often simpler approach to addressing problems involving immigration, as one can see from the proof of the third item of Theorem \ref{thm: DPP2 and similar results for Poisson immigration, such as \cite[Theorem 8]{Mitov-Yanev-Hyrien-18-critical-multi and \cite[Theorem 3]{Rabehasaina-21 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-344: We also have that $D(t\boldsymbol{x )$ tends to 1 $\D \boldsymbol{x}$-a.e., because for $\boldsymbol{x}$ with different entries, $K(tx_i,tx_j) = K(t|x_i-x_j|,0)$ tends to 0 if $x_i \neq x_j$ for $t \to \infty$ by assumption.}
elements.sent-2411-345: Therefore by dominated convergence, \begin{equation}L_{Z}\lbrb{t,\frac{s}{t}} \xrightarrow[t \to \infty]{} 1 + \sum_{n \geq 1}\frac{(-1)^n}{n!} \lbrb{K^\ast\int_0^1\lambda_\infty \beta \frac{\langles, v \rangle}{ 1/Q+(1-x)\langle s, v \rangle}\D x}^n = \lbrb{\frac{1/Q}{1/Q+\langles, v\rangle}}^{K^\ast\lambda_\infty\beta}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-346: The last is indeed the Laplace transform of $Y\boldsymbol{v}$ with $Y\sim \Gamma(K ^\ast\lambda_\infty \beta,1/Q)$ as claimed, which concludes the proof.
